title: Critical Illness Insurance in Canada
seo_title: Critical Illness Insurance Canada | Compare Plans & Quotes | PolicyScanner
seo_description: Compare critical illness insurance in Canada. Get lump-sum coverage for cancer, heart attack, stroke, and 25+ conditions. Ontario-licensed advisors. Free quotes in minutes.

_meta.conversion_goal: Lead capture — get readers to compare CI quotes through PolicyScanner
_meta.page_role: Product pillar page for Critical Illness — sits under life-insurance hub, links to CI vs Disability comparison
_meta.writer_focus: Explain what CI pays (lump sum, tax-free, no strings), what conditions qualify, and who needs it most. Ontario context matters: OHIP doesn't cover lost income or home care during recovery. This is the coverage gap CI fills.

seo_keywords.density_rule: Use 'critical illness insurance' 3–4 times per 500 words. First use on page should be exact phrase. Vary after.
natural_variations: if you were diagnosed with cancer tomorrow
seo_keywords.real_example_to_use: Sara, 42, from Toronto — diagnosed with breast cancer, received $300,000 lump-sum tax-free, used it to pay off mortgage, cover private treatment, and take 18 months off work
seo_keywords.avoid: Do not write 'critical illness insurance canada' as a stiff keyword phrase. Write naturally: 'critical illness insurance in Canada' or 'Canadian CI coverage'.

page_data.heading: Critical Illness Insurance: Get Paid If You're Diagnosed
page_data.subheading: A lump-sum, tax-free payout when you survive a covered diagnosis — cancer, heart attack, stroke, and 25+ more conditions.

_writer_notes.focus: Outcome-first hero. The core promise: you get a cash lump sum if you're diagnosed. Lead with the tax-free, no-restrictions angle.
key_points: Cash goes directly to you — not to doctors or hospitals
Use it for anything: mortgage, treatment, time off work
No OHIP replacement — this fills the financial gap

stats.label: Canadians will develop cancer in their lifetime
Average lost income during a 2-year cancer recovery
Typical survival period before payout triggers
Conditions covered by most CI policies

_writer_notes.focus: Use real Canadian statistics. The '1 in 2' cancer stat comes from Canadian Cancer Society. Income loss figure is from Sun Life research. These should feel urgent, not alarmist.
Plain language explanation. CI pays you a lump sum after you survive a diagnosis of a covered condition for 30 days (the survival period). It's not life insurance — it pays while you're alive.
key_points: Tax-free lump sum paid directly to you — no receipts needed
Triggered by diagnosis + survival period (usually 30 days)
NOT tied to expenses — you choose how to use the money
Most plans cover 25–30 conditions; basic plans cover 3 (cancer, heart attack, stroke)
Different from disability — CI pays once, DI replaces monthly income
_writer_notes.structure: Open with the gap OHIP leaves: you might survive cancer but go broke doing it. Define CI. Distinguish from life insurance and disability. End with the key benefit: freedom to use money however you want.

_writer_notes.focus: List the 3 core conditions (cancer, heart attack, stroke) and then the extended list. Clarify that ALL major insurers cover the Big 3. Express list varies by insurer.
key_points: Core 3: cancer (life-threatening), heart attack, stroke
Extended includes: coronary bypass, kidney failure, MS, Parkinson's, blindness, deafness, organ transplant, aortic surgery, Alzheimer's, coma, paralysis
Some plans cover up to 30+ conditions
Partial benefit: some minor conditions (e.g., early-stage skin cancer) may pay 10–25% of benefit
_writer_notes.structure: Two-tier table: Core 3 (row 1) and Extended conditions (rows 2–15). Note partial benefit column. Note: pre-existing conditions excluded from most plans.

_writer_notes.focus: Step-by-step process from application to payout. Keep it simple and reassuring.
key_points: Step 1: Apply and get approved (medical questions, sometimes exam)
Step 2: Pay monthly or annual premium
Step 3: Get diagnosed with a covered condition
Step 4: Survive the survival period (usually 30 days)
Step 5: Submit claim — insurer pays lump sum within 30–60 days
Step 6: Use money however you want
_writer_notes.real_example: Sara, 42, Toronto — breast cancer diagnosis in February, submitted claim in March after 30-day survival period, received $300,000 in April. Used $150K to pay down mortgage, $80K for private treatment not covered by OHIP, $70K to support family while off work for 16 months.

_writer_notes.focus: Segment the audience. Not everyone has the same need. Help readers self-identify.
key_points: Self-employed: no sick pay, short-term disability, or employer benefits
Single-income households: one earner supporting family — loss of income is catastrophic
People with family history of cancer/heart disease: higher risk, may want coverage earlier
Those with mortgages: CI can pay off or maintain mortgage during recovery
Anyone without 6+ months emergency savings
_writer_notes.structure: Use 4–5 short profiles. 2–3 sentences each. End with: 'If you depend on your paycheque, critical illness insurance protects your financial life when your health fails you.'

page_data.heading: How Much Does Critical Illness Insurance Cost?

_writer_notes.focus: Real monthly cost examples. Non-smoker, healthy, 10-year term CI plan. $100K coverage.
key_points: Age 30, female, non-smoker: ~$35–$55/mo for $100K
Age 35, male, non-smoker: ~$55–$75/mo for $100K
Age 40, female, non-smoker: ~$80–$110/mo for $100K
Age 45, male, non-smoker: ~$130–$180/mo for $100K
Smokers pay 2–3x more
Return of premium option adds 30–50% to cost but refunds premiums if no claim
_writer_notes.structure: Simple table: Age / Male Monthly / Female Monthly. Note: rates vary by insurer, coverage amount, and number of conditions covered. CTA after table.
_writer_notes.callout_after: Add a green callout box: 'These are estimates. Use our quote tool to get your exact rate in 2 minutes — no obligation.'

page_data.heading: Return of Premium: Get Your Money Back If You're Never Sick

_writer_notes.focus: Explain ROP rider — popular in Canada. If you never claim, you get premiums back at expiry or death. It's not an investment but it removes the 'wasted money' objection.
key_points: ROP on expiry: premiums returned at end of term if no claim made
ROP on death: premiums returned to estate if you die without claiming
Costs 30–50% more than non-ROP plan
Makes CI feel like a 'no-lose' product — popular with Canadians
Note: not all insurers offer both ROP options — compare

page_data.heading: Critical Illness vs. Disability Insurance: What's the Difference?

_writer_notes.focus: Key distinction: CI pays once (lump sum on diagnosis). DI replaces monthly income while you can't work. Many Canadians need both.
key_points: CI: lump sum, triggered by diagnosis, use it however you want
DI: monthly income replacement, triggered by inability to work
CI is better for: mortgage payoff, treatment costs, buying time to recover
DI is better for: ongoing bills, multi-year disability, recovery over years
Ideal: have both — they cover different financial risks
_writer_notes.callout_after: Link to: 'Critical Illness vs. Disability — Which One Do You Need?' comparison page

_writer_notes.focus: Ontario-specific context. FSRA regulates CI insurers. OHIP doesn't cover income loss, private treatment, home care, or experimental drugs. CI fills that gap.
key_points: FSRA (Financial Services Regulatory Authority) licenses all CI insurers in Ontario
OHIP does NOT cover: lost income, private clinics, travel for treatment, home care, experimental therapies
Toronto/GTA context: high cost of living makes recovery financially brutal without CI
Ontario advisors are regulated — use a licensed broker for unbiased comparison
PolicyScanner advisors are Ontario-licensed — compare 10+ insurers at once

page_data.heading: Critical Illness Insurance — Frequently Asked Questions

faqs.question: Is critical illness insurance payout taxable in Canada?
faqs.answer: No. Critical illness insurance benefits are paid tax-free when the policy is personally owned and premiums are paid with after-tax dollars. You do not report the payout as income.
faqs._guidance: This is one of the most searched CI questions. Be definitive. Note: if employer pays premiums, it may be taxable — but most personal CI plans are tax-free.

faqs.question: What is the survival period for critical illness insurance?
faqs.answer: Most policies require you to survive 30 days after diagnosis before the benefit is paid. Some conditions (like aortic surgery) may have a shorter or zero survival period. Always check your policy's specific terms.
faqs._guidance: Explain why it exists: prevents claims for immediate death after diagnosis.

faqs.question: Can I get critical illness insurance at 55?
faqs.answer: Yes, though it becomes more expensive with age and some conditions may be excluded based on your health history. Most insurers offer CI up to age 65 for new policies. Getting coverage at 55 is still worthwhile, especially if you have no other coverage.
faqs._guidance: Address the 'am I too old?' concern directly.

faqs.question: Does critical illness insurance cover pre-existing conditions?
faqs.answer: No. If you were diagnosed with a covered condition before applying (or in recent years prior), that condition is usually excluded. Some insurers will decline coverage if the risk is too high. Always disclose fully — non-disclosure can void your claim.
faqs._guidance: Be honest and direct. Don't sugarcoat exclusions.

faqs.question: How many conditions does critical illness insurance cover in Canada?
faqs.answer: Basic plans cover 3 conditions (cancer, heart attack, stroke). Comprehensive plans cover 20–30+ conditions including MS, Parkinson's, kidney failure, blindness, major organ transplant, and more. Always compare the covered conditions list before buying.
faqs._guidance: Help readers understand the product tier differences.

faqs.question: Can I have both critical illness and life insurance?
faqs.answer: Yes — and most financial advisors recommend it. Life insurance pays on death; CI insurance pays if you survive a serious illness. They cover different financial risks and complement each other well.
faqs._guidance: Cross-sell opportunity — link to life insurance hub page.
